steps.title: Fastest path: mark the givens first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Identify given information and angle positioning
steps.content:
We are given JK = MN = 7 cm and \angle J = \angle M = 40 degrees. In triangle JKL, angle J is located at vertex J, positioned between sides JK and JL.

In triangle MNP, angle M is located at vertex M, positioned between sides MN and MP.

To prove congruence, we need one of the standard criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL.

Since we have one side and one angle, we should consider SAS (Side-Angle-Side), which requires two sides with the angle between them.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice against congruence criteria
steps.content:
• "The lengths of sides $KL$ and $NP$ are equal." This gives us JK = MN and \angle J with side KL, which is SSA. The angle J is not between sides JK and KL, so this configuration does not guarantee congruence.
  • "The lengths of sides $JL$ and $MP$ are equal." This gives us JK = MN (side), \angle J = \angle M (angle), and JL = MP (side). Since \angle J is between sides JK and JL, and \angle M is between sides MN and MP, this is the Side-Angle-Side configuration.
  • "The measures of angles $K$ and $L$ are equal." This only provides information about angles within triangle JKL and does not allow direct comparison with triangle MNP.
  • "No additional information is necessary to prove that the two triangles are congruent." One side and one angle are not sufficient for any congruence criterion.
With JL = MP, we have SAS congruence: Side JK = MN, Angle J = M (included between the sides), Side JL = MP. That matches the correct answer - B is correct.
